name: CNH Industrial N.V.

profile.description: CNH Industrial N.V. designs, produces, and sells agricultural and construction equipment worldwide through its Case IH, New Holland Agriculture, STEYR, CASE, and New Holland Construction brands. The company is undergoing a major technology transformation, embedding AI, autonomy, and precision agriculture across its product portfolio. FY2025 revenue was $18.1B (down 8.8% YoY) with net income declining to $386M from $2.5B in FY2024 due to industry downturn and higher input costs. Headquartered in Basildon, UK with 34,197 employees.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): CNH has delivered multiple significant innovation launches including the R4 autonomous farming robot (debuted as proof of concept at Agritechnica 2025), precision planter technology achieving 95% seed placement within 0-5cm accuracy, and AI-driven herbicide reduction systems that won multiple Agritechnica 2025 innovation awards [2][3]. These represent genuine new capability in autonomous farming rather than incremental product refreshes. AI Deployment (1/2): Every member of CNH's global leadership team is running at least one generative or agentic AI pilot program, with CEO Marx stating 'AI is one of the biggest enablers for rapid innovation across our products, people and processes' [2]. Precision tech features are shipping in current equipment but no specific AI-attributable revenue figures are reported. Market Share (2/2): CNH is one of the two dominant global agricultural equipment manufacturers alongside Deere, with Case IH and New Holland brands holding leading positions across North America, Europe, and South America [1]. Product Pipeline (2/2): Announced 2030 strategy to nearly double the share of Precision Tech sales within Agriculture Net Sales, with specific product roadmap including R4 autonomous robot, AI-powered agronomic decision tools, and connected ecosystem platform [2][3]. R&D Intensity (0/1): R&D spending increased in Q1 2026 per earnings report, but as a percentage of revenue remains in line with or slightly below industrial machinery peers.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): CEO Gerrit Marx has articulated a clear purpose: 'Agriculture's biggest challenge is to feed more people, with less land, under increasingly difficult conditions' — connecting CNH's equipment to the global food security challenge [2]. This is specific, meaningful, and differentiates from generic industrial mission language, though it has not been tested across decades of consistent execution under this framing. Community Impact (1/2): CNH has been included in the Dow Jones Sustainability Indices for 11 consecutive years, achieved the highest score of 88/100 among 126 machinery companies in the S&P Global Corporate Sustainability Assessment, and received top marks in environmental policy, human rights, and human capital development [4]. Employee Retention (1/2): 34,197 employees across a simplified matrix organization with leadership team restructured to bring industry-leading experts together. Stable organizational structure, but no specific retention, tenure, or turnover data publicly available. Insider Buying (0/2): No specific net insider buying by CEO Marx or other executives was identified in the last 12 months.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. Strengths include excellent forward P/E of 14.0x (below 20x target) and outstanding forward PEG of 0.6 (well below 1.0 target), reflecting market expectations of earnings recovery from the cyclical trough [5]. Weaknesses are significant: D/E of 333.7% far exceeds the 300% floor (scores 0), reflecting the captive financial services arm typical of equipment manufacturers; ROE of 4.8% is well below the 15% target; profit margin of 2.1% is far below the 20% target; revenue declined 8.8% YoY and 3-year CAGR is -8.4%, both below targets [1][5]. Q1 2026 showed continued pressure with net income of just $10M (down from $132M) as higher tariffs, lower shipment volumes, and increased SG&A/R&D spending compressed margins.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): Agricultural and construction equipment manufacturing is a capital-intensive physical process fundamentally immune to AI displacement — tractors, combines, excavators, and the global dealer/service networks that support them cannot be replicated by software [1]. Physical infrastructure, manufacturing expertise, and regulatory compliance (emissions, safety) create deep structural moats. Moreover, AI is a significant demand enabler: precision agriculture technology increases the value proposition of CNH's equipment, and autonomous farming capabilities create new product tiers and subscription revenue opportunities rather than substitution risk. No analysts flag AI as a disruption threat to CNH's core business. AI Opportunity (1/1): CNH is actively shipping precision technology features in equipment (planter accuracy, herbicide reduction, connected platforms) and has announced a 2030 target to nearly double precision tech's share of agriculture net sales [2][3]. The R4 autonomous robot and agentic AI pilot programs represent tangible AI revenue pipeline.

infinite_game.summary: CNH under CEO Gerrit Marx is making a genuine bet on transforming agricultural equipment from commodity iron to technology-enabled precision farming platforms. The willingness to increase R&D spending during a cyclical downturn (revenue -8.8%), the 2030 precision tech strategy, and the autonomous farming vision all reflect long-term thinking. However, the company's relatively recent formation (spun from Fiat Industrial in 2013) and leadership transitions limit the depth of cause-driven consistency that Sinek prizes.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): CNH's purpose of helping farmers 'feed more people, with less land, under increasingly difficult conditions' is genuine, specific, and connects equipment manufacturing to the global food security challenge [2]. CEO Marx's consistent articulation of this purpose across investor presentations, tech days, and media appearances demonstrates that it shapes strategic decisions — increased R&D spending during a downturn, autonomous farming investment, precision agriculture roadmap. However, the cause is relatively recently articulated and hasn't been tested through multi-decade leadership transitions.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): CNH restructured its Global Leadership Team into a simplified matrix with industry-leading experts, and CEO Marx's mandate that every leader run AI pilot programs demonstrates empowerment and trust in distributed decision-making [2]. The 34,197-employee organization has maintained stability through the industry downturn. However, the significant restructuring itself signals organizational churn, and no specific retention or culture metrics are publicly available.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): CNH explicitly acknowledges John Deere's autonomous farming leadership as competitive motivation, using Deere's strengths to accelerate its own precision tech and AI investment roadmap. The company's competitive framing at Agritechnica 2025 focused on 'customer-centric innovation' rather than defeating rivals, and Marx frames AI as an industry-wide enabler rather than a zero-sum weapon [2][3].

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): CNH's strategic transformation from traditional equipment manufacturer toward a technology-enabled precision agriculture platform represents meaningful business model evolution [2][3]. The 2030 target to nearly double precision tech sales share, R4 autonomous robot development, and AI integration across operations demonstrate willingness to evolve the core business. The shift is incremental rather than transformative — CNH still sells tractors, but the value proposition is changing.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): CNH increased R&D and SG&A spending in Q1 2026 despite a 92% decline in quarterly net income ($10M vs $132M prior year), demonstrating willingness to invest in long-term capabilities (AI, autonomy, precision tech) during a severe cyclical downturn [1]. The 2030 precision tech strategy and R4 autonomous robot investment represent meaningful resource commitments with uncertain payoff timelines.

investment_thesis: CNH Industrial offers compelling cyclical recovery value at 14.0x forward P/E and 0.6x PEG, reflecting market expectations that FY2025's earnings trough ($386M net income, down from $2.5B in FY2024) will reverse as the agricultural equipment cycle normalizes [1][5]. The technology transformation under CEO Marx is genuinely differentiated: the 2030 strategy to nearly double precision tech sales share, R4 autonomous robot, and AI integration across every leadership team's operations position CNH to capture higher-margin, recurring revenue from precision agriculture [2][3]. 11 consecutive years in the DJSI and the industry's highest ESG score (88/100) reflect strong governance and sustainability practices [4]. Q2 2026 guidance calls for agriculture net sales approximately flat YoY with construction up mid-teens percentage.
thesis_continued: The bear case is the severity and duration of the agricultural equipment downturn: revenue has declined from $24.7B in FY2023 to $18.1B in FY2025 (3-year CAGR of -8.4%), net income collapsed 85% from $2.5B to $386M, and Q1 2026 showed continued deterioration to just $10M in net income [1][5]. The D/E of 333.7% reflects the captive financial services arm's leverage but creates balance sheet vulnerability in a prolonged downturn. Profit margin of 2.1% and ROE of 4.8% are far below IOWN targets, and the dividend yield of 0.98% offers minimal income support while the payout ratio of 78% suggests even this modest dividend could face pressure. Construction segment turned to an adjusted EBIT loss of $28M in Q1 2026 as tariffs and weak North/South American volumes compound the challenge [1]. Recovery depends on commodity price normalization and farmer replacement cycles that remain uncertain.
key_catalysts: Agricultural equipment cycle recovery: farmer equipment replacement cycles typically normalize within 2-3 years of downturn, and pent-up demand from aging fleet could drive a sharp V-shaped revenue recovery toward $22B+ levels

Precision technology adoption acceleration: the 2030 strategy to nearly double precision tech sales share within agriculture creates a pathway to structurally higher margins as recurring software/data revenue supplements equipment sales
Construction segment recovery with mid-teens percentage growth guided for Q2 2026 and potential infrastructure spending acceleration in key markets
key_risks: Prolonged agricultural equipment downturn: if commodity prices remain depressed and farmer income does not recover, the revenue decline from $24.7B to $18.1B could extend further, with net income approaching break-even levels

Financial services portfolio risk: D/E of 333.7% reflects captive financing leverage, and rising agricultural loan defaults in a sustained downturn could impair the financial services segment's $74M quarterly net income

Tariff escalation: Q1 2026 results cited higher tariffs as a margin headwind, and further trade policy disruption could increase input costs while reducing export competitiveness in key South American markets

CNH 2025 Tech Day and Agritechnica 2025 Innovation Showcase — CNH Media, November 2025
